content:
## Mar 6, 2026 — Daily Opportunity Scan

### 1. 🔥 NMBR | Dayforce Goes Private — Thoma Bravo Completes $12.3B Acquisition (HIGH VALUE)
- **WHAT:** Thoma Bravo completed its all-cash acquisition of Dayforce (formerly Ceridian) for US$12.3B in February. Combined with the 5% staff cuts, Dayforce is now private and restructuring under PE ownership.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** This is a seismic shift in Canadian payroll. Dayforce going private under PE means cost-cutting, potential product stagnation, and confused customers. PE playbooks prioritize margins over innovation. Nmbr's embedded model becomes more attractive when the incumbent is in transition — partners don't want to bet their payroll on a company mid-restructuring. Customer support will likely degrade further as Thoma Bravo squeezes costs.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Build a "Why Not Dayforce" talking point for partner conversations. Key angle: "They just went private for $12B — their focus is now on returning PE money, not serving mid-market vertical SaaS." This is a window. Use it in the payroll training framework.

### 2. 🎯 CHARLIE | PI Firm Reports 300% Settlement Increase Using AI — Social Proof Gold
- **WHAT:** Houston PI firm John K. Zaid & Associates publicly reported 300% increase in settlements using EvenUp's AI platform for intake automation and case assessment. Multiple press outlets picked it up.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** This is the strongest proof point yet for AI in PI. EvenUp handles post-intake (case assessment, demand letters) — Charlie handles the front door (speed-to-contact, qualification, booking). They're complementary, not competitive. A PI firm using EvenUp for cases is the exact profile that would benefit from Charlie for intake speed.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Reference this case study in Charlie PI outreach: "Firms using AI are seeing 300% better outcomes. But AI inside the case doesn't help if you're losing leads in the first 5 minutes. Charlie makes sure you're first to respond — then EvenUp can take it from there."

### 3. 🏢 NMBR | Softworks Group (Edmonton) Gets Strategic PE Investment — Potential Partner
- **WHAT:** Tributary Group (Chicago PE) invested in Softworks Group, an Edmonton-based regulatory and workforce management SaaS for professional regulators and licensing bodies. Announced Mar 5.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** Softworks is vertical SaaS for regulated industries — exactly the profile of Nmbr's target partners. Post-investment, they'll be looking to expand their platform offering to justify PE returns. Payroll is the natural "and" for workforce management. A workforce management platform that adds embedded payroll becomes stickier.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Research Softworks Group's current product offering and client base. If they serve Canadian professional regulators/licensing bodies, those orgs have employees that need payroll. Could be a partner conversation worth having.

### 4. 📅 NETWORKING | FinteQC 2026 — Montreal, June 2-3
- **WHAT:** FinteQC 2026, Canada's fintech conference, is happening June 2-3 at ESG UQAM in Montreal. Covers fintech innovation, partnerships, and Canadian market trends.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** Montreal is payroll territory (Nethris + Employer D own ~90% of Quebec payroll). This is where Nmbr can plant a flag in Quebec — the hardest Canadian payroll market. Also good for Charlie connections in the legal/coaching fintech space.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Check registration. This is 3 months out — enough time to prep. Quebec payroll expertise is Nmbr's moat; showcasing it at the Quebec fintech conference is smart positioning.

### 5. 💰 DIVIDENDS | National Bank of Canada (NA) Ex-Dividend March 30
- **WHAT:** National Bank of Canada (TSE:NA) goes ex-dividend March 30. Separate from yesterday's picks (Enbridge, Fortis, CIBC), NA is a Canadian bank dividend play that often flies under the radar vs Big Five.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** NA is smaller than the Big Five but has been outperforming on growth, especially with the CWB acquisition. March 30 ex-div gives Drew time to evaluate if he wants to capture this quarter's dividend.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Not financial advice — but if you're looking at Canadian bank dividends beyond CIBC, NA is worth a look before March 30.

---

## Mar 5, 2026 — Daily Opportunity Scan

### 1. 🔥 NMBR | Dayforce (Ceridian) Cuts 5% of Staff — Competitor Weakness (HIGH VALUE)
- **WHAT:** Dayforce (formerly Ceridian) announced ~5% workforce reduction on Feb 12, refocusing operations from Toronto HQ. Meanwhile, their stock analysis shows "smaller customers scrutinizing new IT projects."
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** Dayforce is one of the Big 2 in Canadian payroll (ADP + Dayforce = 70% market). Layoffs + cautious SMB customers = service gaps and unhappy clients. When big players cut staff, their support and onboarding quality drops. That's exactly when mid-market vertical SaaS partners look for alternatives. Nmbr's embedded model sidesteps the "new IT project" scrutiny because payroll is bolted into existing platforms.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Monitor Dayforce customer sentiment on G2/Reddit for complaints about support quality post-layoffs. Could be a talking point for partner sales training — "your customers are getting worse service from the incumbents."

### 2. 🔥 NMBR | Platform Expand Launches Canada Market Entry Services — Channel Partner?
- **WHAT:** Vancouver-based Platform Expand (subsidiary of Sparkcorpnet) just launched three premium services specifically for international companies incorporating in Canada — market entry, incorporation, and trade operations.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** Every international company entering Canada needs payroll. Platform Expand is positioning as the front door for US/international companies expanding north. They're a potential referral channel — they handle incorporation, Nmbr handles the payroll piece for the SaaS platforms these companies use.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Research Platform Expand / Sparkcorpnet. If legit, could be worth a conversation about referral partnerships. Their clients are exactly Nmbr's target profile — companies entering Canada who need compliant payroll fast.

### 3. 📊 NMBR | Deel Prepping 2026-2027 IPO, Doubling Down on Global Payroll Partnerships
- **WHAT:** Deel held a major event emphasizing AI, global payroll partnerships, and go-to-market scaling. IPO expected 2026-2027. Pricing at $29/employee/month for global payroll.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** Deel is a potential competitor AND potential partner. They do EOR/contractor payroll globally but Canadian payroll complexity is a known gap for global platforms. As Deel scales toward IPO, they may seek Canadian payroll partners rather than building it themselves. Alternatively, if they try to build Canadian payroll in-house, that's a competitive signal to watch.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Track Deel's Canadian payroll capabilities. If they're thin on Canada (likely), there may be an integration/partnership play where Nmbr powers Deel's Canadian payroll for SaaS partners.

### 4. 🎯 CHARLIE | Thomson Reuters Report: AI Adoption Doubled in Legal — PI Firms Warming Up
- **WHAT:** Thomson Reuters 2026 AI in Professional Services report shows GenAI use nearly doubled at the organizational level in the past year. They specifically highlight AI for personal injury case workflows via their Supio partnership.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** PI firms are Charlie's target vertical. The fact that AI adoption is doubling means the "AI is scary" objection is dying. Thomson Reuters validating AI for PI specifically makes Charlie's pitch easier — firms are more receptive to AI setters when the big legal tech players are normalizing AI across the practice.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Reference this report in Charlie sales materials. "Thomson Reuters found AI adoption doubled in legal this year — firms using AI for intake and case management are seeing better outcomes. Charlie handles the first critical step: speed-to-contact."

### 5. 💰 DIVIDENDS | March Picks: Enbridge, Fortis, CIBC — Plus Northland Power Watch
- **WHAT:** Motley Fool Canada highlighting Enbridge, Fortis, and CIBC as top March dividend picks for reliability. Separately, Northland Power did a dividend reset and 2026 is their "prove the cash-flow rebuild" year.
- **WHY IT MATTERS:** Enbridge and Fortis are classic boring Canadian dividend plays — right in Drew's lane. Northland Power could be a contrarian entry if the rebuild proves real (higher yield after the reset). CIBC is a solid bank dividend play.
- **SUGGESTED ACTION:** Not financial advice, but worth a look at Northland Power's Q4 earnings when they drop — if cash flow is recovering, the depressed price + reset dividend could be interesting. Enbridge/Fortis remain steady-eddies for the core portfolio.

---

## Mar 4, 2026 — Daily Opportunity Scan (Previous)
- Dayforce layoffs first surfaced
- SaaSpocalypse vertical SaaS positioning
- EOR market growth for Canada entry
